Box A: The Impact of Policy Uncertainty on the Singapore Economy
The Global Economic Policy Uncertainty (GEPU) index (Baker, Bloom, and Davis, 2016)
reached an all-time high in early 2025 (Chart A1), predominantly reflecting a surge in
uncertainty over US trade policy. Apart from the trade-dampening effects of the implemented
tariffs itself, uncertainty around the scale, scope and permanence of trade barriers could also

scope and permanence of trade barriers could also impact business and consumer spending. Firms are likely to hold back on their investments
due to the increased level of uncertainty (Dixit and Pindyck, 1994), while borrowing costs
could also rise with greater risk premia. Meanwhile, households may reduce spending to build
up precautionary buffers against potential income shocks. This Box explores the quantitative
effects of global policy uncertainty on the Singapore economy.

Impulse Response Functions
The impulse response functions (IRFs) from the SVAR model indicate that global policy
uncertainty has statistically significant adverse impacts on the Singapore economy. In
response to a positive one standard deviation (SD) shock to the GEPU index (or a 20.4%

deviation (SD) shock to the GEPU index (or a 20.4% increase in uncertainty), private non-residential investment registers a cumulative 3.6%
decline after four quarters while private consumption falls 1.5% within the same period (Chart
A2). Overall, real GDP is 1.3% below baseline a year after the shock—similar to the 1.0%
estimated by Luk et al. (2017) for Hong Kong but larger than the 0.5% estimated by the
European Commission (2024) for the Eurozone.2

The increase in the EBP in Chart A2 indicates that global credit conditions tighten in
response to higher global policy uncertainty, in line with findings from other studies (e.g.,
Caldara et al., 2016). This affects Singapore’s GDP and points to the presence of a financial
channel through which global policy uncertainty affects real economic activity.

policy uncertainty affects real economic activity. As a robustness check, the IRFs from the SVAR are compared with those from a local
projection model (Jordà, 2005) where the dependent real variables are, separately,
Singapore’s private consumption, private non-residential investment and GDP. The IRFs are
similar; in both models, investment is impacted more greatly than consumption, while the
impact on Singapore’s GDP is greatest four quarters after the shock. Notably, leveraging on

r quarters after the shock. Notably, leveraging on the flexibility enabled by the local projection method, there is evidence of an asymmetry in the
impact of uncertainty. GDP falls by a meaningful extent after a significant increase in
uncertainty but shows no statistically significant response to declines (Chart A3).

Sum-Up
The analysis in this Box suggests that the sharp rise in global policy uncertainty since

the sharp rise in global policy uncertainty since late last year will weigh on Singapore’s growth. Private non-residential investment is likely to
pull back by more than consumption. At the same time, the tightening of global financial
conditions could reinforce the drag on real economic activity in Singapore.

Box B: Macroeconomic Assessment of Budget 2025
This Box item provides an overview and assessment of the key economic initiatives in
Budget 2025.
Macroeconomic Backdrop
Budget 2025 was delivered in February in the context of relative macroeconomic balance,
compared to the previous years of high inflation and volatile growth. At the end of 2024, GDP
was estimated to be slightly above the economy’s potential, while labour market conditions

conomy’s potential, while labour market conditions had largely normalised following a period of tightness. In addition, inflation had fallen
significantly with MAS Core Inflation coming in below 2% y-o-y after an extended period of
elevated inflation.
Nonetheless, elevated costs and prices had remained a concern. In particular, while real
wages in Singapore grew faster over 2020–24 than in many regional economies as well as
some advanced economies, the pace of real wage gains has slowed slightly compared to the

eal wage gains has slowed slightly compared to the pre-pandemic period (2010–19). Further, heightened geopolitical and trade policy uncertainty
posed additional downside risks to Singapore’s small and trade-dependent economy, and
consequently jobs and livelihoods. Indeed, Singapore’s GDP growth is expected to slow in
2025 amid tariff-related weakening in external demand and heightened uncertainty. The
balance of risks was therefore tilted towards growth weakening by more than expected, and
in turn inflation falling more discernibly.

d, and
in turn inflation falling more discernibly. Near-term Support Measures to Alleviate Cost Pressures
Against this backdrop, Budget 2025 sought to address near-term cyclical concerns as
well as longer-term priorities.
The Budget provided further support for coping with cost-of-living pressures, especially
to lower- and middle-income households. For instance, there were additional Community
Development Council (CDC) Vouchers for all Singaporean households, U-Save rebates to help

all Singaporean households, U-Save rebates to help with utilities expenses for eligible HDB households, as well as enhanced support for lower-
income households under the ComCare Assistance schemes.
This year’s Budget also included an SG60 package to recognise the contributions of all
Singaporeans, and to share the benefits of the nation’s progress. The package included SG60
Vouchers for all Singaporeans aged 21 and above this year, as well as a Personal Income Tax

above this year, as well as a Personal Income Tax Rebate of 60% for the Year of Assessment 2025, capped at $200, to mostly benefit middle-
income workers.
In a similar vein to previous Budgets, Budget 2025 also continued to provide support for
firms grappling with higher costs. The Progressive Wage Credit Scheme was enhanced, by
increasing the Government’s co-funding levels for wage increases. A 50% Corporate Income
Tax Rebate was also introduced for the Year of Assessment 2025, capped at $40,000, with

e Year of Assessment 2025, capped at $40,000, with every active company that employed at least one local employee last year receiving a
minimum benefit of $2,000.

e last year receiving a
minimum benefit of $2,000. The Singapore Economy 29
Structural Measures to Sustain Growth and Inclusivity
Aside from addressing near-term concerns, the Budget continued to build on ongoing
efforts to strengthen the supply-side of the economy by enhancing labour quality and building
up the capital stock.
To boost the potential of the resident workforce, the Budget enhanced the Workfare
Skills Support (WSS) scheme, to provide lower-wage workers aged 30 years and above with

de lower-wage workers aged 30 years and above with monthly training allowances when they enrol in selected long-form part-time or full-time
courses. This policy will help to alleviate the sharp income trade-off that lower-wage workers
face when they forgo work to upskill themselves.
To bolster the capital stock, a significant sum ($19.6 billion, 2.6% of GDP) was set aside
to top up funds that will finance spending into the medium term on critical areas such as

ing into the medium term on critical areas such as preparing the nation for climate change, exploring clean energies, investing in productivity
and R&D initiatives, as well as the construction of Changi Airport Terminal 5. The use of fund
top-ups secures the Government’s ability to continue with investments in the Singapore
economy, to support long-term needs in a sustainable way, regardless of shifting cyclical
conditions.
Budget 2025 also enhanced efforts to reduce inequality, support families and improve

to reduce inequality, support families and improve social mobility—important priorities for Singaporeans, as revealed by the Forward Singapore
exercise. The Budget enhanced the Fresh Start Housing Scheme, which helps families with
children living in public rental flats to own their own homes by buying a shorter-lease new flat
with a housing grant. There were also measures to reduce preschool expenses, provide more
support to large families with three or more children, and improve the affordability of long-

e children, and improve the affordability of long- term care expenses. Labour policies that incentivise employers to hire more vulnerable
groups who might experience greater difficulties finding employment, such as seniors, ex-
offenders, and persons with disabilities, were also extended.
Quantitative Assessment of Budget 2025
On a calendar year basis, the fiscal impulse (FI), which gauges the net fiscal support to
GDP from the change in the cyclically adjusted budget balance, is estimated to be a modest

justed budget balance, is estimated to be a modest +0.4% of GDP.1 This is a mildly supportive impulse for the economy in 2025.
To quantify the impact of Budget 2025 in a general equilibrium context and account for
its dynamic effects, key measures were simulated using the Monetary Model of Singapore
(MMS), MAS’ flagship macroeconomic model.
Budget 2025 is estimated to lift real disposable incomes by about 1% in 2025 through
the SG60 package and cost relief measures. This will boost the level of real GDP by about 0.4%

his will boost the level of real GDP by about 0.4% (Chart B1), mainly through stronger consumption growth compared to a counterfactual
scenario without these discretionary fiscal injections.

The impact of Budget 2025 on inflation is expected to be negligible this year and only
modestly positive in 2026 as the delayed transmission from the boost to aggregate demand
this year raises prices incrementally.2 The muted response of inflation to the fiscal stimulus

muted response of inflation to the fiscal stimulus in 2025 also reflects the fact that the bulk of the disbursements are scheduled to be paid out
only in the latter half of this year.
MAS’ simulations also suggest that Budget 2025 will continue to provide some support
to the real economy in the medium term because of measures that seek to lift capital
accumulation and the quality of the workforce. These injections will enable improvements in
aggregate supply by raising firm and worker productivity and in turn, bolster the economy’s

er productivity and in turn, bolster the economy’s productive potential.
All in, Budget 2025 is assessed to maintain an appropriately expansionary impulse amid
the challenging external environment, while supporting longer-term efforts to enhance the
economy’s resilience and competitiveness.

4 Labour Market and Inflation
4.1 Labour Market1
Employment growth slowed amid weaker hiring in the services sectors, while
labour cost increases remained below their recent peaks
In Q4 2024, total employment growth excluding migrant domestic workers (MDWs) stepped
down from the previous quarter. While employment growth in the trade-related sector picked
up alongside the manufacturing upturn in H2 last year, hiring in both the domestic-oriented

H2 last year, hiring in both the domestic-oriented and outward-facing modern services clusters eased (Chart 4.1). In particular, employment
growth in the domestic-oriented services sector slowed to its weakest pace since Q1 2022,
despite the year-end festivities.

measures short-
term change in operating revenue. Labour demand in the services industries was likely weighed down by greater uncertainties
in external growth prospects and subdued consumer spending locally. Persistent softness in
domestic retail and F&B sales led to tepid hiring in these sectors. In addition, headcount in
the Information & Communications (I&C) sector contracted in Q4 on the back of weaker
operating revenue growth, likely as firms in the wider economy saw some consolidation in

rms in the wider economy saw some consolidation in technology investments following robust growth earlier in the year (Chart 4.2).
Early signs of softening in the labour market emerged in the last quarter of 2024, after several
years of tightness. The resident unemployment rate, retrenchment rate as well as the number
of workers placed on short work-week or temporary layoff all ticked up slightly in Q4 2024
from the previous quarter, while the long-term unemployment rate stayed above its historical

term unemployment rate stayed above its historical average. Nevertheless, most indicators remained close to or below their pre-COVID norms,
indicating that as of Q4 2024, the labour market remained in a state of broad demand-supply
balance (Chart 4.3).

appears to be on an increasing trend as firms have possibly been consolidating or economising on their labour inputs. At the same time,

Labour demand could soften further this year, accompanied by slower wage
growth
Aggregate demand for workers is likely to moderate this year in tandem with the cyclical
weakening in the economy. As firms pull back on their hiring and expansion plans amid

pull back on their hiring and expansion plans amid economic uncertainty, employment growth for most sectors should step down from last year.
In particular, the trade-related sectors of manufacturing and wholesale trade could see
greater employment weakness given the increasingly challenging global trade environment.
Nonetheless, labour market adjustments along the extensive margin should mostly impact
non-resident workers. These workers account for a large share of employment in the trade-

ount for a large share of employment in the trade- related sectors. Demand for resident workers in manpower-constrained sectors such as
healthcare should remain resilient. According to the latest ManpowerGroup Employment

4.2 Inflation Developments
Inflation slowed further and came in below expectations in Q1
MAS Core Inflation fell significantly to 0.6% y-o-y in Q1 from 1.9% in Q4 2024, while CPI-All
Items inflation moderated to 1.0% from 1.4% (Chart 4.7). In both instances, the pace of price
increases was weaker than expected. The decline in core inflation in Q1 compared to the
previous quarter was driven to a large extent by a broad-based weakening in underlying price

ent by a broad-based weakening in underlying price pressures.
The low inflation prints reflect a strengthening of the disinflation trend that began in mid-
2024. By Q4 last year, inflation momentum had fallen to low levels. It has since weakened
further, turning negative in Q1 (Chart 4.8). Alternative measures of underlying inflation that
abstract from more volatile price components also indicated continued disinflation.

in the decade before the pandemic. Recent outturns appear more similar to the episode in

correspondingly, in inflation pressures (Table 4.1). By contrast, the low inflation in 2015 was

e 4.1). By contrast, the low inflation in 2015 was more narrowly based, primarily driven by a large fall in energy- and travel-related prices due to
sharply lower crude oil prices. While the weighted median inflation is higher than in the
preceding two episodes as at Q1 this year, it remained significantly below its historical
average.

Continuing lacklustre consumer spending locally was likely a factor dampening inflation.
Retail and F&B sales volumes have been tepid for several quarters, with the latter remaining
well below pre-COVID levels (Chart 4.9). Pressures on real wage growth as well as some
expenditure switching abroad due to the elevated S$ real effective exchange rate (S$REER)
have likely been factors dampening consumption expenditure in Singapore. Moreover,

ng consumption expenditure in Singapore. Moreover, competitive pressures in sectors such as F&B may have increased, reducing the ability of
firms to pass through cost increases.3
Chart 4.9: Retail and F&B Sales Volumes Chart 4.10: Import Price Indices
Source: DOS Source: DOS
Meanwhile, business cost pressures have also remained relatively modest. In recent months,
Singapore’s oil and non-oil import prices have continued to fall on a y-o-y basis, lowering the

e continued to fall on a y-o-y basis, lowering the cost of utilities and imported intermediate goods (Chart 4.10). While the cost of food imports
rose, this was driven mainly by sharp increases in a narrow group of items such as coffee and
cocoa, the prices of which have risen by over 30% y-o-y in recent months amid poor harvests
globally. Excluding these items, food import prices have risen more modestly by 2.8% y-o-y in
Jan–Feb. On the domestic front, commercial rents for retail spaces have also stayed broadly

l rents for retail spaces have also stayed broadly flat since 2021, while services ULC grew at a moderate pace of 1.4% y-o-y in 2024, in line with
its historical average.
Against this backdrop, inflation fell across a broad range of CPI categories in Q1. Retail &
other goods prices continued to register outright declines from year-ago levels while
discretionary services inflation eased. Notably, inflation for food services and essential
services both fell below their historical norms with the latter partly reflecting enhanced

l norms with the latter partly reflecting enhanced government subsidies. Overall, inflation rates for most broad components of the CPI basket
are now below their historical averages (Chart 4.11).

Both MAS Core and CPI-All Items inflation are now projected to average 0.5–1.5% in 2025.
The downgrade in the core inflation forecast, from 1.0–2.0% previously, reflects the weaker-
than-expected inflation outturns in Q1 as well as expectations that domestic demand and

1 as well as expectations that domestic demand and business cost pressures will come in more moderate than previously anticipated (Chart 4.12).
The CPI-All Items inflation forecast range has also been lowered, from 1.5–2.5% previously,
due to both the step-down in the core inflation forecast and softer-than-anticipated headline
inflation at the beginning of the year.

On balance, higher global tariffs are expected to exert broad-based disinflationary impulses.
While the US imposition of higher import duties on nearly all its trading partners will lift
imported inflation in the US, its passthrough to consumer prices locally should be limited by
Singapore’s relatively modest imports of consumer goods from the US. At the same time,
some global exports originally destined for the US may be redirected to countries that have

or the US may be redirected to countries that have not raised import duties, such as Singapore, increasing the supply of goods and lowering
import prices for the domestic economy. Further, weaker global aggregate demand will weigh
on the prices of commodities and manufactured goods, exerting demand-driven
disinflationary pressure on Singapore’s import costs.
With the downgrade in Singapore’s GDP growth forecast, domestic demand and cost
pressures are also expected to moderate. The easing in aggregate labour demand will exert

. The easing in aggregate labour demand will exert a drag on nominal wage growth and thus discretionary spending, which, together with the
projected negative output gap, will further dampen inflationary pressures. Growth in services
ULC (adjusted by trend productivity) is also forecast to be slower than last year, and below its
historical average.
All in, the anticipated slippage in global and domestic demand will temper inflation across
several broad CPI components for the year as a whole. In particular, falling global crude oil

s a whole. In particular, falling global crude oil prices will cause electricity and gas prices to decline, while the anticipated below-trend
services ULC growth will dampen discretionary and essential services inflation (Chart 4.13).
Cost passthrough to prices should also be mitigated by soft consumer demand.
The risks to the inflation outlook are tilted to the downside. The near-term disinflationary
effects of higher global tariffs for Singapore could intensify if the associated negative
ngapore could intensify if the associated negative demand effects turn out to be larger or more long-lasting than expected.

economy.

Preface
The main chapters of this Review provide a comprehensive analysis of
the conjunctural developments and outlook for the global and
domestic economies. These are integral components of MAS’
monetary policy decisions.
In this issue of the Review, EPG is pleased to present Special Feature
A, which provides a select review of the 11th Asian Monetary Policy
Forum held on 23–24 May this year in Singapore. The Special Feature

24 May this year in Singapore. The Special Feature was contributed by Choy Keen Meng, a consultant to EPG. We thank
Scott Roger, Advisor to EPG, for contributing Special Feature B, which
reflects on the global experience with the inflation targeting framework
widely adopted among major central banks today. We are grateful to
Tang Heiwai, the Victor and William Fung Professor in Economics at
the University of Hong Kong Business School and Director of Asia
Global Institute, for contributing Special Feature C. The article reviews

ontributing Special Feature C. The article reviews the history of globalisation since the 19th century, and discusses how
it is changing amid rising geopolitical tensions and increased
economic fragmentation. We are also pleased to present Box A, in
collaboration with the ASEAN+3 Macroeconomic Research Office
(AMRO), which examines the impact of a carbon tax hike in the
ASEAN+3 economies. Finally, our appreciation goes to Kodjovi M.
Eklou from the IMF for contributing Box B, which estimates the

he IMF for contributing Box B, which estimates the exchange rate pass-through to inflation in Singapore.

Monetary Policy Statement
INTRODUCTION
1. In the July 2024 Monetary Policy Statement, MAS maintained the rate of appreciation of the
Singapore dollar nominal effective exchange rate (S$NEER) policy band, with no change to the width
of the band or the level at which it was centred. Since then, the S$NEER has been appreciating
gradually in line with the policy band.

2. The global economy has remained broadly resilient. Growth has been steady in the US,
sustained by domestic demand, but has been weaker in the Euro area. In China, exports were buoyant
in the first half of 2024, but domestic spending continued to be subdued amid the housing market
downturn. Regional economies meanwhile have benefited from the sustained upturn in the global

benefited from the sustained upturn in the global electronics cycle, with investments supported by tech-related activities.
3. Singapore’s major trading partners are expected to stay on a steady expansion path in the
quarters ahead. Final demand will be underpinned by further declines in interest rates as inflation
moderates worldwide. Recent policy interventions should also help stabilise near-term growth in
China.

4. According to MTI’s Advance Estimates, the Singapore economy expanded by 2.1% on a
quarter-on-quarter seasonally-adjusted basis in Q3, accelerating from the average of 0.4% in the first
half of the year. Growth momentum was stronger than expected, and was largely underpinned by a
step-up in manufacturing output, particularly in the electronics industry. Activity also picked up in the
modern services cluster.

ity also picked up in the
modern services cluster. 5. For the rest of 2024, Singapore’s growth should be sustained by the ongoing upswing in the
electronics and trade cycles as well as the easing in global financial conditions. For the year as a
whole, MAS expects GDP growth to come in around the upper end of the 2–3% forecast range. The
negative output gap is projected to close in H2 2024.
6. Next year, the Singapore economy is currently forecast to expand at close to its potential rate.

forecast to expand at close to its potential rate. However, there is significant uncertainty around the economic outlook, reflecting continuing risks in
the external environment. A sharp escalation in geopolitical and trade conflicts could exert sizeable
drags on global and domestic investment and trade. There is also uncertainty around the pace and
impact of global macroeconomic policy easing, and with it, the durability of the electronics upturn.
INFLATION OUTLOOK

ility of the electronics upturn.
INFLATION OUTLOOK 7. MAS Core Inflation1 stepped down to 2.6% y-o-y in Jul–Aug, from 3.0% in Q2, as consumer
price increases fell across a broad range of goods and services. Excluding the impact of the GST
hikes, core inflation is estimated to have edged below 2.0% in Jul–Aug. The seasonally adjusted
three-month/three-month rate2 of core inflation also declined to an annualised rate of 0.9% in August,
reflecting a slower pace of price increases in food services, including hawker food, as well as a range

ervices, including hawker food, as well as a range of retail and household durable goods.
8. Core inflation momentum is expected to remain contained in Q4, which would imply a further
slowing in its y-o-y rate over the next few months. Core inflation should end the year around 2%, and
average between 2.5%–3.0% for 2024 as a whole, down from 4.2% in 2023. CPI-All Items inflation
fell to 2.3% y-o-y in Jul–Aug and should come in around 2.5% this year, compared to 4.8% in 2023.

n around 2.5% this year, compared to 4.8% in 2023. 9. Amid moderate underlying cost pressures, MAS Core Inflation is expected to average around
the mid-point of the forecast range of 1.5–2.5% in 2025. Imported costs are forecast to be broadly
stable next year, reflecting an anticipated unwinding of oil production cuts and favourable weather
conditions for food supply. CPI inflation in Singapore’s major trading partners should also largely be
in line with pre-pandemic norms. On the domestic front, unit labour costs are projected to rise more

ront, unit labour costs are projected to rise more gradually, alongside moderating nominal wage growth as well as a recovery in productivity.
10. CPI-All Items inflation is forecast to average 1.5–2.5% as well in 2025. Accommodation
inflation should slow as leasing demand falls, partly offsetting an anticipated pickup in private
transport inflation amid still-firm car purchases.
11. The risks to Singapore’s inflation outlook are more balanced compared to three months ago.

normalise. An intensification of geopolitical tensions and commodity price shocks could add to

MONETARY POLICY
12. Singapore’s growth momentum has picked up and the negative output gap is projected to
close in H2 2024. Barring a weakening in global final demand, the economy should continue to

lobal final demand, the economy should continue to expand at a steady pace and keep close to its potential path in 2025. MAS Core Inflation has stepped
down but is anticipated to decline further to around 2% by the end of 2024.
13. Based on this outlook, MAS assesses that the monetary policy settings are for now still
consistent with medium-term price stability.
14. MAS will therefore maintain the prevailing rate of appreciation of the S$NEER policy band.
There will be no change to its width and the level at which it is centred.

1 The International Economy
• The global economy has remained broadly resilient since the April Review.
Most of Singapore’s key trading partners saw an increase in growth
momentum in Q2. In the region, despite China’s growth slowdown, the
ASEAN economies were buoyed by the ongoing tech cycle recovery, as
well as firmer domestic demand. Meanwhile, global disinflation has
progressed further in recent months, with services inflation finally easing

ent months, with services inflation finally easing meaningfully alongside a moderation in labour cost pressures.
• Barring major shocks and discontinuities, the last mile of disinflation
should remain on track in 2025, alongside steady growth in most
economies. Aggregate demand should continue to expand, underpinned
by firm household spending and business investment, with additional
support coming from the near-synchronised easing of global monetary
policy.
• Nonetheless, uncertainties on both growth and inflation are elevated.

ainties on both growth and inflation are elevated. Geopolitical events arising from potential escalation of the ongoing
military conflicts and trade tensions pose heightened risks to growth. On
the inflation front, a significant and sustained escalation in the Middle East
conflict that disrupts oil production and shipping routes could impart fresh
impulses to inflation.

Global disinflation has progressed as services inflation finally showed signs
of easing
Global disinflation made further progress since the April Review. Both headline and core
inflation edged down to 2.1% in Jul–Aug, from 2.3% and 2.2% in Q2, respectively. This easing
was supported by several factors. First, the price of Brent crude oil fell to US$80 per barrel
(/b) in August, from US$90/b in April. Second, shipping and logistical costs have fallen from

nd, shipping and logistical costs have fallen from their peaks earlier in the year when geopolitical tensions in the Middle East initially escalated.
Third, and more importantly, services inflation in the advanced economies (AEs) finally
showed signs of slowing. An earlier key impediment to the disinflation process had been
sticky services inflation amid the tight labour market (Chart 1.5). With the recent moderation
in wage growth in the US and Eurozone, labour cost pressures have eased accordingly and

, labour cost pressures have eased accordingly and helped facilitate the broader cooling of services inflation (Chart 1.6).

1.2 Global Outlook
The global economy should continue to expand at a steady pace in 2025
In the quarters ahead, growth in the AEs will continue to be driven by domestic demand,
particularly in the US. Private consumption will be supported by improving real wage growth
as inflation falls back to central banks’ targets. In the US, business investment should remain

gets. In the US, business investment should remain strong, undergirded by the IT refresh cycle as firms seek to adopt AI-related technologies. In
Japan, the June Tankan survey also signalled strong investment intentions by manufacturing
firms for the rest of the year, after having been postponed due to the pandemic as well as
high import prices of capital goods. Most AE central banks have commenced policy easing
in recent months, which will help to support the economic expansion over 2025.

help to support the economic expansion over 2025. Among ASEAN economies, growth should improve next year. Industrial production in
these economies has maintained a steady pace of expansion in recent months (Chart 1.7).
The broadening of the tech cycle upturn from AI-related applications to consumer electronics
and PCs should continue to benefit Asian economies into 2025. (Refer to Section 2.2 for more

while strong productivity growth will continue to support non-inflationary wage increases.

ousing supply shortages should ensure that shelter inflation does not reaccelerate, notwithstanding some monthly volatility. In the Eurozone,

could also partially absorb the higher wage costs.

Despite the benign outlook, uncertainties on both growth and inflation remain
elevated
The global economy is confronting heightened geopolitical conflicts and trade tensions
that could induce fresh shocks to growth and inflation.
Ongoing geopolitical conflicts pose downside risks to the global growth outlook. The
Geopolitical Risk Index2, which provides a measure of the frequency of adverse geopolitical

a measure of the frequency of adverse geopolitical events and associated risks based on news articles, remains elevated relative to
pre-pandemic levels (Chart 1.11). Further escalation of the Middle East conflict or the
Russia-Ukraine war could weigh on household and business confidence. At the same time,
disruptions to oil production and shipping routes through the Middle East, arising from a
significant and sustained escalation in the conflict, could impart pressures on global producer
prices and inflation.

pressures on global producer
prices and inflation. Likewise, trade tensions are on the rise and could derail the disinflation process. The
Trade Policy Uncertainty Index3, which compiles the frequency of trade policy and uncertainty
terms across major newspapers, has been rising since late 2023, and is currently at its highest
level since early-2020 (Chart 1.12). An intensification of tensions could lead to further trade
frictions and potential retaliatory actions that weigh on global trade and output, while raising

at weigh on global trade and output, while raising prices. Central banks may have to slow the pace of monetary policy easing amid renewed
near-term inflationary pressures.

Box A: Carbon Pricing in Asia1
Introduction
Climate change caused by rising concentrations of greenhouse gases poses a
significant challenge to modern societies and economies, creating a need for joint action to
limit carbon emissions. Carbon pricing is a potentially effective policy tool to help facilitate
the needed changes in fossil fuel energy use.
Carbon pricing has not been as widely adopted in Asia as in some other parts of the

dely adopted in Asia as in some other parts of the world, due in part to implementation challenges and concerns over its potential impact. This
Box analyses how higher carbon prices in Asia would affect specific industries, through both
direct and indirect channels. While the estimated economic impact could be non-trivial,
simulations using a global macro model show that other policies can be adopted to mitigate
some of the negative near-term effects.
Current State of Carbon Pricing in Asia

m effects.
Current State of Carbon Pricing in Asia Countries in Asia have adopted various approaches to carbon pricing. The most
common is via an emissions trading system (ETS), which China, Japan, Korea, and Indonesia
have adopted. China operates both a national ETS, covering the power sector, and eight
regional ETS pilots that focus on other parts of the economy, including transport, industry,
and buildings. Korea has a national ETS covering sectors such as waste, aviation, industry,

overing sectors such as waste, aviation, industry, and power. In addition to its two subnational ETS in Tokyo and Saitama, Japan implemented
a new voluntary ETS in October 2023, which is expected to transition into a mandatory
national ETS in 2026. In ASEAN, Indonesia recently launched its ETS in 2023. While this
scheme currently covers only coal-fired power plants, Indonesia aims to expand its scope
over time.
In comparison to an ETS, carbon taxes have been less widely used across Asia, but

taxes have been less widely used across Asia, but interest in this approach is gaining traction. Japan introduced a carbon tax in 2012, but the
rate has remained very low, at around USD 2 per tonne of CO2-equivalent (tCO2e). Singapore
was the first in ASEAN to implement a carbon tax in 2019, with plans to increase the current
rate of USD 20/tCO2e gradually in the next six years, to reach USD 38–60/tCO2e by 2030.
Thailand recently announced the imposition of a carbon tax of about USD 5.5/tCO2e from

sition of a carbon tax of about USD 5.5/tCO2e from 2025—only on oil products for now, such as diesel and gasoline—making it the second ASEAN
economy to utilise this instrument. Indonesia had also planned to introduce a carbon tax in
2022 but postponed the implementation to 2025 due to the still-weak global environment and
volatile energy prices at that time (Teja, 2023).
Other countries in the region―including Malaysia, the Philippines, and Vietnam―are
currently assessing or planning the introduction of carbon pricing instruments to align with

uction of carbon pricing instruments to align with their commitments to the Paris Agreement. Malaysia will introduce a carbon tax for the iron
and steel industries by 2026, while Vietnam plans to launch a pilot ETS on a voluntary basis
in 2026. Thailand’s draft Climate Change Act, once approved, will also formally establish the
rules and mechanisms for a domestic ETS. Among smaller ASEAN economies, Brunei and

In addition to these explicit carbon pricing schemes, many countries in the region—such
as Indonesia, Lao PDR, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand and Vietnam—are already

Assessing the Impact of Carbon Pricing on Industries in Asia

the Impact of Carbon Pricing on Industries in Asia In general, higher carbon prices raise the cost of fossil fuel-based energy and thereby
production costs in an economy. The higher relative costs of such fossil fuels should
incentivise substitution to greener energy alternatives and more energy efficient technologies.
At the same time, the increase in production costs has a negative income effect and could
weigh on the output of the affected industry sector in the short term, which would cascade
through the economy as a whole.

hich would cascade
through the economy as a whole. The analytical exercise below shows how a more extensive adoption of carbon pricing
across Asia would feed through industry linkages using an input-output (IO) framework. This
is an important perspective as sectors face differing costs from the implementation of carbon
taxes, depending on the impact on their production structures. Based on how much of this
cost burden is passed on to output prices, certain commodities or services will also

prices, certain commodities or services will also experience a fall in demand as the market moves towards less carbon-intensive substitutes.
Carbon Tax Assumptions and Country Coverage
To conduct this analysis, country-level carbon tax rates are computed from the Net Zero
2050 scenario provided by the Network of Central Banks and Supervisors for Greening the
Financial System (NGFS).2 This is NGFS’ most ambitious scenario, where net CO2 emissions
reach zero around 2050 through stringent polices, and global warming is limited to 1.5⁰

ent polices, and global warming is limited to 1.5⁰ Celsius. In this scenario, carbon prices are assumed to increase by between USD 160 and
USD 315 per tonne of CO2e in nine Asian economies in the short run,3 depending on the
country. The nine economies (Asia-9) examined can be grouped into the five middle-income
economies—China, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand (henceforth MI-5)—and
the four high-income economies—Hong Kong, Korea, Japan and Singapore (henceforth HI-4).
Input Output Analysis: Methodology

nceforth HI-4).
Input Output Analysis: Methodology Leontief input-output analysis is employed to calculate a first-order estimate of the
sectoral and economy-wide effects of the carbon taxes. This linear approach4 provides useful

315/tCO2e. To be consistent with the Net Zero 2050 scenario, these are assumed to be in place by end-2026.

tax shift.
In the first step, estimating the price impact from implementing the Net Zero 2050
scenario requires an assumption on the post-carbon tax change in factor costs (Δ𝐟′), for each
industry. The increase in factor costs arising from the tax hike can be approximated by
multiplying the new carbon prices with industry-specific CO2e emissions levels for each

h industry-specific CO2e emissions levels for each economy. Sector-level carbon tax rates are computed to provide an initial estimate of the
increase in the cost of production.
In the next step of the procedure, the presumed carbon tax is incorporated in a vector of
the changes in factor costs (Δ𝐟′) for each industry.5 Using the Leontief input-output table of
coefficients, 𝐀, the change in prices (Δ𝐩′) of output of each industry is computed as:
Δ𝐩′ =Δ𝐟′⋅[𝐈−𝐀]−1 (1)

each industry is computed as:
Δ𝐩′ =Δ𝐟′⋅[𝐈−𝐀]−1 (1) where 𝐈 is the diagonal identity matrix. Demand elasticity assumptions were imposed to
calculate the vector of the effects of the imputed price changes on final demand (Δ𝐱). Then
the vector of industry-by-industry changes in total output (Δ𝐪) (and therefore in total revenues)
can be calculated as:
Δ𝐪′ =[𝐈−𝐀]−1⋅Δ𝐱 (2)
The analysis was implemented across 35 industries for Asia-9 economies, which
includes 14 industries in manufacturing, 19 in services and the remaining covering agriculture

in services and the remaining covering agriculture and mining. This follows the structure of the Asian Development Bank’s (2023) Multiregional
Input-Output Tables, from which the latest available data for 2022 were used for this exercise.
Input-Output Analysis: Results
Accounting for about 80% of Asia-9’s CO2 emissions, the utility sector will face the
highest average effective carbon tax rates under the Net Zero scenario (Chart A1). This is
followed by the transport sector, particularly aviation and shipping. The initial, substantial

ly aviation and shipping. The initial, substantial increase in producer prices will be primarily concentrated in a few sectors, mostly within the
five most carbon-intensive industries. Nevertheless, the overall increase in prices is primarily
driven by the carbon tax hike’s indirect impact, rather than the direct rise in factor costs (Chart
A2). The tight industry linkages in Asia-9 will play a key role in transmitting the impact of
carbon prices to the rest of the economy via higher intermediate input costs.6 Clearly,

omy via higher intermediate input costs.6 Clearly, transition costs can spread beyond individual industries, with economy-wide implications.

and so a new tax policy—in this case, a carbon price hike—will induce an increase in factor costs.

For most normal goods, price increases will translate into lower demand for a sector’s

es will translate into lower demand for a sector’s goods or services, and consequently, revenues. Lower profits can weigh on the affected
sector’s demand for goods from other upstream sectors, depending on inter-industry
production linkages. To gauge which Asia-9 sectors could face the largest revenue declines,
two different demand scenarios are examined: (1) a moderate decline in demand (MS) and
(2) a severe fall in demand (SS). The two differ in terms of price elasticity assumptions to

differ in terms of price elasticity assumptions to help illustrate a range of potential industry output losses as a result of a carbon tax,
considering the different degrees of price elasticity of demand for different goods and
services. The first scenario assumes demand is inelastic except for a subset of industries
that produce goods or services whose demand is more discretionary in nature, which are
assumed to have unitary elastic demand. The second scenario extends this assumption of
unitary elastic demand to all sectors.7

umption of
unitary elastic demand to all sectors.7 Under a moderate demand shock scenario, the large revenue losses from both direct
and indirect effects will cut across Asia-9 manufacturing—especially the transport equipment,
furniture, and electronics industries—as well as services, especially aviation (Charts A3 and
A4). In MI-5, services related to tourism and travel, as well as to transport equipment could
face significant revenue declines, especially as the transportation sector remains as one of

lly as the transportation sector remains as one of the largest emitters in the region. Under the severe scenario where final demand for all
sectors is equally responsive to the carbon price increase, the most carbon-intensive
industries would experience the largest revenue losses: utilities, transport services, and the
manufacturing of fuels, minerals, metals, and chemicals. Revenue losses across Asia-9 could
be up to 1.5 times higher in the case where indirect effects through the supply chain are fully
considered (Chart A5).

considered (Chart A5). It is crucial to note that the resulting IO estimates can only be interpreted as the first-
order, short-run effects of any tax policy, i.e., it does not consider substitution in response to

Policies to Mitigate the Impact of Carbon Pricing
Notwithstanding the upper-bound negative impact presented above, there are
complementary policies that governments in the region can adopt to mitigate the effects of
adopting a transition policy centred on carbon pricing.
To assess these policy options, an integrated global model, Oxford Economics’ Global
Economic Model (GEM) is used. The GEM is a global, general equilibrium model with

he GEM is a global, general equilibrium model with Keynesian features in the short run and neo-classical properties in the long run. It contains
carbon tax levers that generate fiscal revenues, alongside fiscal and other policy instruments
that can be adjusted. Being a global model, it also captures trade and financial linkages
between economies.8 However, it is important to note that the longer-term substitution
effects and production process changes that would arise due to higher carbon prices are not

at would arise due to higher carbon prices are not endogenously captured in the GEM. Three key observations emerged from the model
simulations.
First, policies to use the additional revenues collected from the carbon tax, also known
as “revenue recycling”, can substantially offset the drag on output from higher carbon taxes.
For instance, governments could increase transfers to households, boosting private
disposable incomes and household spending, or increase government investment, which also

ing, or increase government investment, which also has positive externalities of inducing increases in private investment, especially in many
emerging Asian economies. Model simulations show that if the additional revenue from
carbon taxes is fully recycled, it can offset around 55–65% of the impact that carbon taxes
alone could potentially have on aggregate output. The offset could be larger if the fiscal
spending is specifically targeted at initiatives such as public infrastructure that would support

s such as public infrastructure that would support green investment or household transfers to encourage the purchase of more energy-efficient
appliances.
Second, if Asia-9 economies also pursue a drive to boost renewable capacity as carbon
taxes induce higher demand for renewable sources of energy, overall energy prices should
fall and support output in the economy. There is some evidence that public spending in green
investments can raise private investment through positive externalities and market creation

through positive externalities and market creation effects. Deleidi et al. (2019) investigated the effect of public direct investment on private
investment in renewable electricity technologies for 17 countries over 2004–14 and found
that public investments not only had a positive but also consistently the largest effect on
private investment flows relative to feed-in tariffs, taxes, and renewable portfolio standards.
Model simulations show that a 50% increase in electricity output from renewable sources will

in electricity output from renewable sources will support output modestly, offsetting about 15% of the drag from carbon taxes alone. An
efficient way to boost the supply of renewable energy is by importing it, particularly for
resource-constrained economies (such as Hong Kong and Singapore) with limited renewable
energy options domestically.
Third, the additional fiscal spending that is enabled through recycling carbon tax
revenues also creates positive spillovers to other economies via trade linkages. In a

lovers to other economies via trade linkages. In a simulation where all Asia-9 economies implement a combination of higher public investment
and household transfers as described above, the additional final domestic demand supports
the exports of trading partners, including outside of Asia. This stimulates second-round
8 The simulations were undertaken in the version of the GEM with a ten-year forecast horizon. For more details
on the GEM, please refer to Oxford Economics (2021) and Oxford Economics (2022).

ford Economics (2021) and Oxford Economics (2022). 22 Macroeconomic Review | October 2024
spillback effects to economies implementing carbon taxes with recycling. The simulation
shows that such spillovers could help offset the drag from carbon taxes by as much as 20%.
Conclusion
This Box highlights the potential impact on output of carbon pricing in Asia-9, particularly
for industries and economies that are currently more reliant on carbon-intensive energy

currently more reliant on carbon-intensive energy sources. Even as the incidence of carbon pricing may initially fall disproportionately on a few
key sectors, the impact will cascade through production interlinkages, further affecting
sectors not directly impacted by the initial energy price shock. As such, the use of climate
transition policies—undoubtedly urgently required—would need to be sensitive to both the
direct and indirect sectoral and distributional effects, while still preserving the important price

ffects, while still preserving the important price signal embodied in carbon prices that would help economies move towards net zero. This
will be especially crucial for economies with a large number of small- and medium-sized
enterprises in the supply of intermediate goods or services, which would have less capacity
to react to sizeable price shocks.
More importantly, the negative impact of carbon pricing on an economy’s output can be
mitigated to a significant extent by the design of accompanying government policies and

the design of accompanying government policies and measures. Recycling carbon tax revenue as household transfers and government investment
are useful levers in this domain. There are also considerable positive spillovers across the
region if more economies adopt similar policies, including those that support investment in
renewable energy capacity. Ultimately, a clear and predictable timeline of policy
implementation and regional cooperation would help to manage both the impact and risks of
the climate transition that must be undertaken.

increased trading activity in the financial sector, and the return of Chinese tourists.

Overall, the Singapore economy gained a firmer footing, with close to 60% of
the economy exceeding or at pre-pandemic growth norms
GDP outturns have improved discernibly, progressing from below average2 growth in
2023 (1.1%), to around average in H1 2024 (3.0%) and above average in Q3 2024 (4.1%). The
share of industries (based on nominal GDP values) recording below average growth declined

DP values) recording below average growth declined from around 70% in 2023, to 50% in H1 2024 and 40% in Q3 2024 (Chart 2.12). As such, close
to 60% of the industries recorded average and above growth by Q3. The improvement in 2024
relative to 2023 was partly attributed to a turnaround in the external-facing sectors such as
wholesale trade, water transport & storage and finance & insurance. It was however the
recovery in the manufacturing sector that was most evident in Q3 2024, as it registered above

as most evident in Q3 2024, as it registered above average growth, following below average outturns throughout 2023 and H1 2024.

2.2 DOMESTIC OUTLOOK
The generally conducive external environment will support the Singapore
economy’s expansion in 2025
The past few years have been eventful for the Singapore economy, from the tumultuous
years of the pandemic with movement restrictions and subsequent reopening (2020–21), to
weakness in manufacturing and financial activities amid the global tech downturn and high

activities amid the global tech downturn and high global interest rate environment (2022–23). This year, after a somewhat lacklustre first half,
the economy is now expanding more definitively.
Growth for the rest of 2024 and into 2025 should continue to be supported by the
ongoing upswing in the global tech cycle, alongside the gradual easing of financial conditions.
Based on forecasts from the IMF, most of Singapore’s major trading partners are expected to

Singapore’s major trading partners are expected to post generally stable growth in 2025, in line with that of 2024 (Chart 2.13). Although growth
in the US and China is expected to moderate in 2025, improved prospects in the Eurozone
and several ASEAN economies should provide some countervailing support.

Recovery in the global goods trade and the tech cycle should continue apace
next year
The outlook for global trade remains generally sanguine. According to the World Trade
Organisation, global merchandise trade volume is expected to expand by 2.7% in 2024, and
pick up further to 3.0% in 2025.
Similarly, the global tech cycle recovery should continue, broadening beyond AI-related
infrastructure demand (e.g., data centres) to consumer devices amid the ongoing

data centres) to consumer devices amid the ongoing replacement cycle. Global chip sales grew by 23% y-o-y in Jul–Aug, extending the 18%
increase in H1 2024, led by continued strong sales in the memory chip segment. PC and
smartphone shipments have also turned around from last year and expanded by 0.5% and 6%
y-o-y in Q1–Q3 2024, respectively. Growth is likely to pick up further from end-2024 as
manufacturers ramp up shipments of AI-enabled PCs and smartphones. Meanwhile,

ents of AI-enabled PCs and smartphones. Meanwhile, investment demand for information processing equipment in the US accelerated in Q2 to 4.7%
y-o-y, following a modest increase in the previous quarter (Chart 2.14). Retail sales for IT
products in Singapore’s top two final markets, China and the US, also grew at a firmer pace in
recent quarters (Chart 2.15).

The World Semiconductor Trade Statistics expects global chip sales to expand by 16%
in 2024, before moderating slightly to 12.5% in 2025. Notably, the memory chip segment is
expected to post strong outturns in the quarters ahead, supported by still-elevated prices

quarters ahead, supported by still-elevated prices amid increased memory content in AI-enabled PCs and smartphones, and the rapid adoption
of high bandwidth memory (HBM) to meet rising demand for faster processors required by AI
servers. However, chips related to the automotive & industrial end markets are projected to
decline this year, amid surplus inventory and sluggish demand.

Beyond the trade-related cluster, the increased adoption of AI should also provide a
tailwind for the information & communications sector as domestic firms ramp up their
digitalisation plans. For instance, Seatrium, a large offshore marine engineering firm, is
partnering with M1 to integrate 5G technology within their shipyards to allow for more

echnology within their shipyards to allow for more advanced digital capabilities such as smart video analytics.3 Likewise, Singtel is collaborating
with South Korean telecommunications provider SK Telecom to explore innovative solutions
that leverage AI as it progresses towards 6G technology.4 Consequently, the push for
digitalisation should drive demand for data and cloud services and strengthen growth of the
sector. Notably, Amazon Web Services intends to spend an additional $12 billion from 2024

tends to spend an additional $12 billion from 2024 to 2028 on enhancing its local cloud infrastructure to cater to the growing demand for its
cloud services. The company will also be launching a flagship AI programme to accelerate
the adoption of AI by enterprises in Singapore.
A further key support is the pipeline of digital initiatives by the public sector. The
government plans to spend an estimated budget of $3.3 billion on ICT procurements in FY24,
of which more than half will be channelled to enhancing digital infrastructure and improving

institutions would receive $200 million of funding support from the government over the next
five years to better use AI tools to automate routine operations and to ramp up preventive
care for patients.6 Further, government policies such as the Refundable Investment Credit
scheme and the Green Data Centre Roadmap should incentivise firms to expand their ICT-

Global monetary policy easing is expected to gather pace in 2025 and support

ing is expected to gather pace in 2025 and support financial sector growth
Within the financial sector, activity in the fund management segment should remain firm,
supported by asset reallocation trades and higher AUMs. Institutional and retail investors are
likely to rebalance their portfolios from less-risky cash-equivalent assets to equities and
bonds amid the global easing in monetary policy. At the same time, AUMs of fund managers
should continue to grow as international investors increase their allocation to Asia in view of

stors increase their allocation to Asia in view of the region’s steady growth prospects as well as for portfolio diversification.
The banking sector’s growth performance should remain healthy even as declining
global interest rates weigh on banks’ net interest margins. Net interest incomes are expected
to stay resilient, underpinned by a pickup in credit demand and reallocation towards fixed-
rate assets. Overall profits should also be supported by non-interest income, particularly net

supported by non-interest income, particularly net fees and commissions from wealth management amid increased mandates and portfolio
rebalancing.
Meanwhile, the insurance segment may see higher gross premiums as existing products
are repriced to improve profit sustainability. The payments segment should grow alongside
greater promotional and marketing efforts to attract retail customers.
Against this backdrop, the Singapore economy is likely to expand around its
potential rate in 2024 and 2025

expand around its
potential rate in 2024 and 2025 Overall, growth in the Singapore economy is likely to come in around the upper end of
the 2–3% forecast range in 2024, and be broadly similar next year. GDP growth in both years
is expected to be underpinned by the trade-related and modern services clusters.
While near-term prospects are firm, the outlook for 2025 is less certain. The outcome of
the upcoming US Presidential Elections, an escalation in geopolitical tensions including in the

calation in geopolitical tensions including in the Middle East, or a sharper slowdown in China could adversely affect global trade and growth,
and in turn weigh on Singapore’s economic prospects. Additionally, the durability of the AI-
led global tech cycle recovery remains uncertain and could be sensitive to aggregate demand
conditions.

2.3 ASEAN’s Role in the Electronics Value Chain amid a
Shifting Geopolitical Landscape
This section provides an update8 on geoeconomic fragmentation and reconfigurations in the
electronics global value chain since 2021, and explores further complementarities between
Singapore and the broader ASEAN region amid these shifts.
Trade between the US and China continues to be diverted through “connector
countries”9, especially in the electronics value chain

tries”9, especially in the electronics value chain The world has been in a “slowbalisation” phase since 2009, characterised by a slower
pace of global economic integration. This trend did not change significantly despite ongoing
geopolitical tensions. After the onset of the US-China trade war, global merchandise exports
continued to fluctuate around a range of 20–25% of global GDP from 2018 to 2023, similar to
that in the preceding period of 2009–2017 (Chart 2.17). Special Feature C provides an

9–2017 (Chart 2.17). Special Feature C provides an overview of the history of globalisation since the 19th century.

Foreign firms are de-risking and pursuing a China+1 strategy, with ASEAN and
India playing an intermediary (production) role
The emergence of the “connector” economies stems from the adoption of a dual supply
chain strategy by many large global firms. In essence, these firms are producing in China for
the domestic market, and outside of China for the rest of the world. This marks a shift from

for the rest of the world. This marks a shift from the past, when multinational firms channelled foreign direct investment into China for
production to serve both its domestic and global markets.
In 2021–23, the value of announced greenfield investments in the global electronics
sector quadrupled to an average of US$200 billion from its 2015–2020 levels. However, the
value of announced global greenfield projects in China has generally trended down over this
period (Charts 2.22 and 2.23). In contrast, the ASEAN economies and India have seen

contrast, the ASEAN economies and India have seen substantial FDI inflows in recent years, particularly in the electronics industry. Notably,
companies such as Foxconn have expanded their operations in Malaysia, focusing on the
production of electronic components, device assembly, and semiconductor packaging and
testing. Samsung and LG Electronics have also made significant investments in Vietnam, to
manufacture mobile phones and home appliances. Similarly, Apple has increased its share of

ances. Similarly, Apple has increased its share of iPhone production in India. Apple assembled 14% of its iPhones in India in fiscal year 2024,
doubling from the year before.
Charts 2.22 and 2.23 The value of announced greenfield FDI projects to China has been subdued in
recent years as foreign firms pivoted their supply chains to ASEAN and India, particularly in the

ASEAN will continue to play a key role in the global electronics landscape, and there is
scope for greater specialisation and collaboration within the region. For instance, Singapore
and Malaysia have a comparative advantage in the upstream and midstream electronics

dvantage in the upstream and midstream electronics value chain, such as the production of semiconductor equipment and integrated circuits
(Chart 2.27). This complements countries such as Vietnam, which specialise in the final
assembly of consumer products, such as smartphones, further downstream in the value

Singapore is plugged into the electronics value chain through the production and export
of intermediate goods to the region, including China and ASEAN. It remains an attractive

ncluding China and ASEAN. It remains an attractive location for high value-added semiconductor manufacturing, with recent projects by Siltronic
and VisionPower Semiconductor Manufacturing Company among others. As foreign
multinationals continue to pivot towards ASEAN, Singapore should see indirect spillovers
from increased electronics production in the region, through higher demand for upstream and
midstream components.

In sum, recent trade and foreign direct investment trends indicate that ASEAN has
benefited from the reconfiguration in the electronics value chain in the near term. To fully
capitalise on new growth opportunities, regional economies could deepen trade agreements,
press on with structural reforms, and invest in human capital and infrastructure, including by
adopting AI technologies. Singapore could continue to anchor advanced manufacturing

re could continue to anchor advanced manufacturing activity and enhance its linkages with the region, by leveraging its strategic re-export role and
providing supporting services such as transportation, financial and consulting services. This
collaborative approach bolsters regional connectivity and efficiency, positioning ASEAN as a
key player in the evolving global manufacturing and trade landscape.

3 Labour Market and Inflation
• In the first half of 2024, the domestic labour market continued to ease
relative to last year, as hiring slowed further. Nonetheless, the
unemployment rate remained low amid stable levels of involuntary job
separations. Nominal wage growth also slowed, albeit from a high base,
across more sectors in the economy.
• The ongoing upturn in economic activity should support job creation in the

onomic activity should support job creation in the quarters ahead. Nevertheless, labour demand is unlikely to be strongly
resurgent, as the GDP growth pickup is largely being driven by capital-
intensive sectors. In addition, some evidence of labour hoarding amid
structural constraints on labour supply should put a cap on the pace of
employment expansion.
• Nominal wage growth is expected to ease further in 2025, but will settle
slightly above the pre-COVID norm on account of manpower constraints

pre-COVID norm on account of manpower constraints and policies to raise the relative wages of lower income workers. Labour
productivity growth is expected to improve this year and next, which would
temper the impact of continuing wage cost increases and help ensure
stronger real wage gains.
• Inflation continued to decline steadily in Q3, amid a marked deceleration
in the pace of price increases for both goods and services. Lower imported
fuel and food costs, due in part to the appreciating S$NEER, as well as

due in part to the appreciating S$NEER, as well as slowing unit labour cost increases contributed to the fall in inflation.
Alternative measures of underlying inflation in the economy affirm the
broad-based nature of the disinflationary trend, which likely indicate that
accumulated costs have been almost fully passed through to prices. As
such, absent fresh cost impulses, y-o-y core inflation is expected to decline
further to reach around 2% by the end of 2024.
• In 2025, core and headline inflation are both expected to average

nd headline inflation are both expected to average 1.5–2.5%, compared to the 2024 forecast of 2.5–3.0% and around 2.5%,
respectively. Inflation is expected to be lower next year as cost pressures
stay contained. Commodity prices and global inflation remain moderate
while growth in unit labour cost for the services sector should slow further.
The latter, together with enhanced government subsidies for essential
services to temper cost of living pressures, will dampen services inflation.

living pressures, will dampen services inflation. The upside risks to inflation are now broadly countervailed by the growing
possibility of inflation coming in below 2%, such that risks to inflation have
become more balanced.

that risks to inflation have
become more balanced. Labour Market and Inflation 41
3.1 Labour Market1
Employment growth moderated in the first half of 2024, indicating that
previous tightness in the labour market continued to ease
Total employment growth excluding migrant domestic workers (MDWs) and the
construction sector weakened further in Q2 2024, continuing its moderating trend since Q3
2022 (Chart 3.1).2 The moderation in job gains during the first half of this year was in line with

uring the first half of this year was in line with weaker economic conditions. Resident employment contracted slightly in Q2, although this
partly reflected seasonal and one-off factors. In particular, these were the annual retraction
of festive season hiring in the domestic-services sectors, as well as the fading of temporary
demand for workers following a number of major events in Q1.
Overall, labour market indicators point towards some easing in labour demand in H1

s point towards some easing in labour demand in H1 relative to last year, when hiring sentiment was notably strong. Recruitment and resignation
rates fell further from below-average rates the previous quarter, as employers hired less
aggressively and job-hopping behaviour for workers in search of better prospects declined.
On a seasonally adjusted basis, recruitment rates were lower than their pre-COVID average in
most sectors of the economy in Q2 (Chart 3.2). The re-entry rate of retrenched residents to

3.2). The re-entry rate of retrenched residents to employment also declined further, indicating that jobseekers could be taking longer to find
jobs. This likely contributed to long-term unemployment, which refers to unemployment spells
of 25 weeks or more, staying slightly above the pre-COVID norm in H1 this year.

Worker supply constraints have kept involuntary job losses and
unemployment low
Nevertheless, the resident unemployment rate continued to stay low, falling from 3.0%

ment rate continued to stay low, falling from 3.0% in March to 2.7% in June this year. One reason is that employers have been reluctant to
release existing workers, possibly given difficulties of replacing them with similarly-skilled
hires amid labour supply constraints. Firms may also have looked past the temporary soft
patch in economic activity. Consequently, the number of workers seeing involuntary job
losses or reduction in hours has remained in line with their 2015–19 averages, in turn keeping

line with their 2015–19 averages, in turn keeping the number of unemployed residents low (Chart 3.3).
Moreover, recent tightening of several foreign worker policies has likely helped to
support demand for resident workers. For example, qualifying salaries for S Pass Holders
(SPHs) were raised in September 2022, September 2023, and September 2024, while that of

The recent upturn in growth should lead to some recovery in hiring
Over the near term, moderately supportive demand conditions should keep the labour
market close to full employment. After declining for several quarters, recent employment

declining for several quarters, recent employment surveys indicate that hiring sentiment may be improving (Chart 3.6). DOS’ Business
Expectations survey for the services sector shows an uptick in employment expansion plans
for Q3, driven by the tourism-related sectors. Manpower Group’s employment outlook also
indicates that labour demand could strengthen further by the end of this year, led by stronger
hiring in the financial services and information & communications sectors.

services and information & communications sectors. In addition, employers’ financial conditions appear to have strengthened in recent
quarters, which will provide some basis for a pickup in job creation. Gross operating surplus
growth turned around in H1 2024 after four consecutive quarters of negative growth in 2023.
At the same time, growth in compensation of employees has moderated, amid lower
compensation per worker and employment growth (Chart 3.7). Overall, hiring should be

ment growth (Chart 3.7). Overall, hiring should be supported in the coming quarters as wage growth moderates, and employers see profitability
and business prospects improve. The firming economic activity should thus provide some
impetus to labour demand and put a floor to any weakness in the labour market.

The unemployment rate should remain relatively low
Resident unemployment is likely to remain low amid the near-term stabilising of labour
demand. Nevertheless, there may be some emerging mismatch between available jobs and
the skills/expectations of resident jobseekers, which could lead to a risk of workers being
underemployed. Time-related underemployment could have risen in H1 this year, as average

yment could have risen in H1 this year, as average weekly hours worked dipped below trend across many sectors.4 In addition, the re-entry rate
into employment for retrenched PMETs and older workers is now lower than the pre-COVID
average, indicating that some groups of workers may be having greater difficulty securing
employment that matches their skills.5

3.2 Consumer Price Developments
Core inflation fell further amid a broad-based easing of price increases
MAS Core Inflation eased to 2.7% y-o-y in Q3 2024 from 3.0% in Q2, while CPI-All Items

-y in Q3 2024 from 3.0% in Q2, while CPI-All Items inflation fell to 2.2% from 2.8% over the same period. Cumulatively, core and headline inflation
have fallen significantly from their respective peaks of 5.4% in Q1 2023 and 7.3% in Q3 2022.
Excluding the impact of the GST hikes over the past two years, core inflation is estimated to
have slowed to 2.0% in Q3, the lowest reading since Q4 2021.
The decline in core inflation was underpinned by a marked deceleration in the pace of

nderpinned by a marked deceleration in the pace of price increases across both goods and services (Chart 3.10). Around two-thirds of the core
consumption basket registered sequential inflation below their historical averages in Q3, a
proportion last seen in the pre-pandemic years. Alternative measures of underlying inflation,
such as the trimmed mean inflation rates, also affirm that the easing momentum is broad-
based and not driven by the more volatile CPI components (Chart 3.11). Momentum in the

atile CPI components (Chart 3.11). Momentum in the persistence-weighted inflation measure, which places greater weight on items for which cost
impulses take longer to dissipate, has also fallen significantly.

Moderating cost pressures and soft demand continued to drive disinflation
Lower inflation in recent months was predominantly driven by an easing of business cost
pressures. Singapore’s imported costs have continued to moderate on the back of lower
global inflation and the strengthening S$NEER (Chart 3.12). On a year-ago basis, global prices
of energy have fallen due to mounting concerns of weaker underlying global demand. Prices

oncerns of weaker underlying global demand. Prices of key food commodities have also stayed below year-ago levels amid ample supply
conditions. Meanwhile, the appreciating S$ exchange rate continued to significantly dampen
import and consumer prices. (See Box B on an empirical analysis of exchange rate pass-
through to inflation in Singapore.)
As demand and supply in input and output markets have come into better balance in
recent months, wage and price increases in the Singapore economy have steadily moderated.

in the Singapore economy have steadily moderated. The slower rise in wage growth, alongside increasing productivity, has contributed to
disinflation, especially for services. Meanwhile, consumer expenditure domestically has been
weak, with retail (excluding motor vehicles) and F&B sales volumes still falling in y-o-y terms
in Jul–Aug. This has likely restrained the pace at which firms can raise consumer prices.
All in, the aggregate core price level has largely caught up with earlier accumulated cost

as largely caught up with earlier accumulated cost increases and firms’ markups are estimated to have largely normalised (Chart 3.13). With the
pass-through of prior cost increases to consumer prices diminishing as a driver of inflationary
pressures, core inflation has moderated.

In the near term, subdued upstream cost pressures should continue to keep a lid on
domestic inflation. With producer price inflation still weak in major goods-producing
economies such as China and the Eurozone, increases in Singapore’s manufactured product

one, increases in Singapore’s manufactured product and supply prices have remained relatively contained, and are unlikely to exert any significant
upside pressure on consumer prices (Chart 3.16).
Global commodity prices are also projected to decline or remain broadly stable in 2025.
Although crude oil prices were volatile in October following the intensification of the conflict
in the Middle East, the extent of increase has been limited. This is partly because global oil

as been limited. This is partly because global oil supply is expected to be supported by the anticipated unwinding of OPEC+’s production cuts
from end-2024.6 In addition, the US$ has broadly depreciated, which should ease cost
pressures on commodity importers globally. More moderate energy costs as well as the
weaker US$ should also cap increases in global food prices. At the same time, fertiliser prices
have already fallen sharply, and favourable weather conditions should support grain

favourable weather conditions should support grain production. Together, this should reduce the cost of feed and keep meat and dairy price
pressures contained.
